narrativeRiskCallouts: NH3-N summer exceedances (Jun–Sep 2025: 2.2–2.4 mg/L vs 2.0 mg/L limit) are now four consecutive months of documented NPDES violations. The state permitting authority has this pattern on file. Summer 2026 is on track to repeat or worsen without aeration intervention.

BOD and TSS are running with thin headroom (BOD peaks at 23 mg/L vs 25 mg/L limit; TSS peaks at 28 mg/L vs 30 mg/L limit in multiple months) — a secondary treatment capacity conversation may be warranted in parallel.

whatThisIs.insight: Four consecutive months of summer 2025 NPDES NH3-N exceedances on a documented upward trend — the state has this on file, and summer 2026 is on course to repeat without aeration intervention.
whatThisIs.body: A ~2 MGD conventional activated sludge POTW serving roughly 28,000 people is failing its summer NH3-N permit limit (2.0 mg/L) for the first time on record, with June–September 2025 readings of 2.2, 2.3, 2.3, and 2.4 mg/L. The 2024 summer peaked at 1.8 mg/L — under the limit but with no margin. The year-over-year worsening is unambiguous. The plant superintendent has already identified aging blower equipment as the likely cause, which is consistent with the eDMR pattern: insufficient dissolved oxygen in the aeration basins limits nitrifier activity precisely when summer temperatures elevate ammonia loads. BOD and TSS remain within permit limits but are trending toward the ceiling in several months, suggesting the secondary treatment process is running at the edge of its design envelope more broadly. The budget authority (utility director or public works director) has not yet entered the conversation — the superintendent is the engaged contact, but the capital decision will require someone above him.

whatWeWouldPropose.insight: A planned aeration upgrade (blower replacement + DO control optimisation) commissioned before summer 2026 costs roughly the same as emergency replacement under an NOV — but eliminates the penalty, rental, and procurement premium that makes the deferred path materially more expensive.
whatWeWouldPropose.recommendedApproach: Aeration system scoping and upgrade for a ~2 MGD CAS plant: (1) DO profiling and blower capacity audit to confirm equipment configuration and diffuser condition; (2) high-efficiency blower replacement (turbo or multistage centrifugal) with automatic DO control (dissolved oxygen sensor + VFD-based turndown), sized for current 2 MGD design flow with headroom to ~2.5–3 MGD; (3) fine-bubble diffuser grid assessment and replacement if diffuser fouling is contributing to low transfer efficiency; (4) O&M protocol optimisation (MLSS management, RAS rate discipline, SRT control) as low-/no-capex measures to restore nitrification margin while capital project delivers.

winWinArguments.lead: Permanent summer compliance — not seasonal luck.
winWinArguments.body: The 2024 summer data showed the plant running at 1.8 mg/L with no margin. Summer 2025 broke through the limit four months running. Summer 2026 is on course to be worse unless aeration capacity is restored. A properly sized aeration upgrade delivers the DO profile nitrifying bacteria need, restoring predictable compliance before the next permit season — not dependence on cool weather or reduced loading to squeak through.

winWinArguments.lead: NOV and enforcement schedule avoided — while the utility still controls the timeline.
winWinArguments.body: A documented pattern of consecutive NPDES exceedances puts the utility on a trajectory toward a formal NOV or informal compliance schedule. Once that clock starts, corrective action is accelerated, penalties compound, and the capital project is executed on the regulator's timeline — not the utility's. Acting in the next capital cycle keeps the utility in the driver's seat on scope, procurement, and schedule.

winWinArguments.lead: Planned procurement vs emergency replacement — same equipment, materially different total cost.
winWinArguments.body: A planned blower replacement with proper scoping, competitive procurement, and CWSRF financing takes 12–18 months from scope to commissioning. An emergency replacement under an NOV-driven compliance schedule is procured without competitive bidding leverage — typically at 20–40% premium — and usually requires temporary rental aeration (~$15K–40K/month for a plant this size) while permanent equipment is fabricated and installed. The cost of deferral isn't zero; it's a procurement premium plus rental plus penalties.

costOfAlternativeRows.component: Rental aeration during NOV-driven emergency replacement (est. 3–6 months @ ~$20K/mo avg)
NOV / NPDES penalty exposure (summer 2026 repeat, 4+ months; typical municipal POTW state fines $5K–$25K/event + $1K–$10K/day)
Emergency procurement premium (no competitive bid, 20–40% over planned equivalent)
Ongoing exceedance exposure, summers 2026–2028 (consent order risk, ECHO public listing, permit reissuance at tighter limits)

whatWeWouldPropose.dealSizeSensitivity: Range driver: number of blower units (not yet in evidence) and whether diffuser grid replacement is included (+20–35%). CWSRF financing available for eligible projects — reduces effective cost of capital without changing scope. MEDIUM-LOW confidence; site visit needed to pin the range.

whatCouldKillIt.insight: The equipment-only frame is the kill risk — if procurement goes straight to a blower vendor before we get to the utility director with the regulatory argument, we lose the positioning battle before it starts.

risks.name: Equipment-only frame wins
risks.mechanism: Superintendent recommends a direct like-for-like blower swap to the public works director. Procurement goes to an equipment vendor or existing maintenance contractor. The process engineering and DO control optimisation that actually ensures permit compliance never enters the conversation — and neither do we.
risks.mitigation: Get to the utility director / public works director with the permit-exceedance framing before the equipment-vendor conversation advances. The eDMR pattern is the wedge — equipment vendors don't bring it. Lead with the regulatory trajectory, not the technology.

risks.name: Regulatory escalation stalls
risks.mechanism: State regulator issues a soft warning or takes no visible action on summer 2025 exceedances, reducing perceived urgency through the winter months. Superintendent deprioritises the project; budget authority never engages.
risks.mitigation: Anchor the urgency argument on summer 2026 exposure and the documented trend — not whether the state has acted yet. The eDMR record is objective; the state's enforcement lag doesn't erase it.

risks.name: Capital budget cycle closes before we reach decision-maker
risks.mechanism: Municipal capital budgets cycle on fiscal-year schedules. If the utility director or council doesn't have this project in the FY2026 capital plan before the budget window closes, the deal slips 12 months — and the regulatory situation likely worsens before the next cycle.
risks.mitigation: Identify the budget cycle close date in the next conversation. Target the utility director directly before that window. Frame this as a project that needs to be in the next cycle to commission before summer 2026.

doThisNext.insight: The superintendent is your entry point but not your closing contact. The deal advances when the utility director sees the regulatory trajectory, not when the superintendent confirms the blowers are old.

actions.title: Surface the regulatory posture
actions.body: Ask the superintendent directly: has the state permitting authority been notified about the summer 2025 exceedances, and have they indicated what corrective action or timeline they expect? The answer tells you how much urgency already exists at the authority level — and whether an NOV is closer than the superintendent realises. This is your most important qualifying question.

actions.title: Get a site visit committed + confirm blower configuration
actions.body: Request a plant walk to confirm blower make, model, age, and basin layout. Diffuser condition and DO profile across the aeration train are the key scoping variables. A site visit also moves the conversation from 'we're aware of the problem' to 'we're actively scoping a solution' — which is the Scope stage gate.

actions.title: Identify and engage the budget authority
actions.body: Confirm who approves a capital project of this size — utility board, city council, or public works director acting independently. Get the fiscal year budget close date. If FY2026 capital planning is still open, the utility director needs a brief one-pager on the regulatory trajectory and project cost framing before that window closes. If it's already closed, understand when FY2027 opens.

finalText:
**Field Brief is ready.** Here's the link: [📄 Download Field Brief PDF](?threadId=eval-edge-case-long-edmr-evidence-field-brief-only-gFqnmY2z0CSY4cAZp82XF&kind=field-brief)

---

**Read this first — the headline:**

The eDMR data tells a sharper story than "aging blowers." This utility posted four consecutive months of summer NH3-N exceedances in 2025 (peak 2.4 mg/L vs a 2.0 mg/L limit), following a 2024 summer that peaked at 1.8 mg/L with no margin. The year-over-year worsening is unambiguous and the state has it on file. Summer 2026 is on course to repeat without intervention.

**The cost-of-alternative bottom line:** deferring or patching converts a planned $800K–$2.5M aeration upgrade into a reactive spend of roughly the same magnitude *plus* an estimated $250K–$900K in NOV penalties, rental aeration, and emergency procurement premium — plus an uncapped consent-order tail if the pattern continues. Acting in the next capital cycle costs the same money with the risk removed.

**Stage: Qualify / MEDIUM confidence.** The superintendent is engaged and has made the equipment diagnosis. The budget authority (utility director / public works director) hasn't entered the conversation yet, and the regulatory posture (whether the state has moved on the 2025 exceedances) is unknown. Those two gaps are what the brief's three actions are built around.
